briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for July 11, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global and Domestic Heatwave Crisis
sections.content: Today, South Korea is facing a severe heatwave, with perceived temperatures around 33 degrees Celsius and heatwave advisories expanded across all of Seoul [19, 20, 21]. Notably, there have been shocking reports of over 10,000 deaths per week due to heatwaves domestically, and record-high temperatures continue, with Hayang-eup, Gyeongsan, soaring to 37.9 degrees Celsius [1, 19]. These heatwaves are not merely a domestic issue. Across Europe, extreme heat is also threatening human lives and social functions, with temperatures on the London Underground exceeding the legal limit for transporting cattle, and some classroom temperatures surpassing 40 degrees Celsius, causing students to suffer from heatstroke [38, 39, 40, 41]. This once again reminds us that climate change poses an urgent and direct threat to human survival and daily life worldwide.

sections.title: ⚠️ Threats to Ecosystems and Social Infrastructure Caused by Climate Change
sections.content: Climate change is having widespread and catastrophic impacts not only on human societies but also on global ecosystems. Biodiversity is under threat, with Pacific gray whales experiencing 'catastrophic' mass die-offs due to impacts on food supply, and invasive northern pike causing severe damage to Alaska's salmon populations [22, 25]. Furthermore, scientific warnings continue that climate change is causing fundamental changes to ecosystems at both microscopic and macroscopic levels, such as shrinking squid brains and altering the chemical properties of the Arctic [29, 33]. It is also directly impacting local economies and cultures, with ski lift closures at French ski resorts and damage to scallop production in Japan, raising fundamental questions about whether our infrastructure is adequately prepared for the effects of these climate changes [26, 34, 35].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Efforts and Policy Progress in Responding to Climate Change
sections.content: Amidst these crises, various domestic and international efforts and positive progress in responding to climate change continue. Domestically, Suseong-gu Mayor Kim Dae-kwon invited a German delegation to discuss climate change cooperation, and the Korea Forest Service strengthened forest cooperation for desertification prevention and climate crisis response, leveraging the Korea-Mongolia summit [2, 4, 5]. In various sectors of society, the spread of carbon neutrality practices and ESG management are being emphasized, with the European medical community adopting carbon neutrality in clinical settings and the Masan Free Trade Zone embarking on a full-scale transition to carbon neutrality [6, 7, 9, 10]. Internationally, policy and legal responses are also being actively discussed, with the International Court of Justice expected to issue an advisory opinion on climate change, and the necessity for progress on climate change being emphasized at this conference [24, 28]. Furthermore, the Hawaii State Teachers Association providing global climate change training and research indicating that Himalayan hydropower shows remarkable resilience to future climate change scenarios offer hope through education and technological innovation [27, 37].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Efforts and Challenges for Renewable Energy Expansion and Stabilization
sections.content: In the renewable energy sector, a core pillar of climate crisis response, positive developments coexist with challenges that need to be addressed. The news that the renewable energy market size has surpassed 10 trillion dollars, growing into a key pillar of the global economy and the third-largest industry, is encouraging [17, 18]. Domestically, the expansion of renewable energy businesses is accelerating, with Hanwha Ocean commencing the construction of wind turbine installation vessels and Hanwha Q CELLS building a super-large renewable energy complex in Arizona, USA [11, 13]. The government and Korea Midland Power are working to secure 1GW of renewable energy and alleviate grid saturation through projects supporting the introduction and establishment of ESS in distribution networks [12, 15, 16]. However, questions are also being raised regarding the grid and management readiness for renewable energy as it expands into 'community-friendly' applications [14]. This suggests that beyond mere expansion, stable grid integration and efficient management of renewable energy are critical challenges for the next phase.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the urgency of the heatwaves sweeping across the globe and the widespread threat of climate change, while also demonstrating the international community's ongoing multifaceted efforts to overcome these challenges. Accelerating the transition to renewable energy and establishing stable systems are urgent priorities, and the importance of education and cooperation in responding to the climate crisis is growing ever greater. This concludes the climate briefing for July 11, 2026.

articles.title: ‘Like a sauna’: London tube travellers swelter in temperatures higher than legal limit for cattle
articles.description: The tube cannot easily be adapted to cope with heatwaves, making conditions almost unbearableAs the escalator descends below ground at King’s Cross St Pancras station in London, the shift from what was already a hot station entrance to the furnace-like subterranean depths is perceptible.On the tube it’s worse: a man leans back in his seat, eyes closed, sweltering; people hold electric fans an inch away from their faces. London commuters are known for their stoicism and the heat appears to be ano

summary.phenomenon: Passengers are suffering from extreme heat in the London King's Cross St Pancras station underground and on the Tube, with temperatures exceeding the legal limit for cattle.
summary.cause: This is due to the Tube system being designed in a way that makes it difficult to cope with heatwaves, leading to very high temperatures in the deep underground sections.
